title: Anharmonic lattice dynamics and superconductivity in strained bulk and surface niobium
authors: Mihir Ranjan Sahoo, Roman Lucrezi, Pedro Nunes Ferreira, Chia-Nien Tsai, Matthew Julian, Rohit P. Prasankumar, Mahmoud I. Hussein, Christoph Heil
abstract: Using first-principles calculations, we investigate how homogeneous strain and crystallographic surface orientation modify the vibrational and superconducting properties of niobium. For bulk Nb, tensile strain strongly softens the phonon spectrum and enhances the electron--phonon coupling, increasing the superconducting transition temperature from 9.5 K at equilibrium to 14.5 K at $\sim\!6\%$ lattice expansion. For the low-index Nb(001), Nb(110), and Nb(111) surfaces, harmonic phonon calculations exhibit imaginary modes, showing that anharmonic lattice effects are essential. To treat these effects efficiently, we train Nb-specific machine-learning interatomic potentials on bulk and slab first-principles configurations and use them to accelerate stochastic self-consistent harmonic approximation calculations, thereby obtaining anharmonically renormalized phonon modes that are combined with density-functional perturbation theory electron--phonon matrix elements to construct the Eliashberg spectral function. Among the clean free-standing slabs considered here, Nb(001) exhibits the strongest electron--phonon coupling and the highest calculated transition temperature of 10.0 K, while Nb(110) and Nb(111) show progressively reduced pairing strength. Finally, by analyzing the Eliashberg spectral function and the functional derivative $δT_\text{c}/δα^2F(ω)$, we identify the phonon energy ranges most effective for superconducting pairing. Our results show that strain, surface termination, and anharmonic phonon renormalization provide complementary and interrelated microscopic routes for tuning superconductivity in Nb.
summary: This work uses first-principles calculations to map out how strain and surface termination tune superconductivity in Niobium. By incorporating anharmonic lattice dynamics, the authors show that tensile strain boosts $T_c$ in the bulk, and the (001) surface provides the best pairing strength among the analyzed terminations. The study highlights the crucial, interconnected roles of lattice anharmonicity and dimensionality in superconducting material design.
main_problem: The study investigates how homogeneous strain and crystallographic surface orientation modify the vibrational and superconducting properties of niobium (Nb).
main_result: Tensile strain significantly enhances the superconducting transition temperature ($T_c$) in bulk Nb, while the $	ext{Nb}(001)$ surface exhibits the highest $T_c$ among clean slabs, demonstrating that strain, surface effects, and anharmonicity are key tuning mechanisms.
method: The research employs first-principles calculations (DFT) combined with advanced lattice dynamics treatments, including Machine-Learning Interatomic Potentials (MLIP) accelerated Stochastic Self-Consistent Harmonic Approximation (SSCHA) to capture anharmonic effects.
model_or_system: The physical system is Niobium (Nb), analyzed in both its bulk form and as clean, free-standing ultra-thin slabs with (001), (110), and (111) terminations. Superconductivity is modeled using the electron-phonon coupling (EPC) framework.
key_observables: Superconducting transition temperature ($T_c$), electron-phonon coupling constant ($\lambda$), Eliashberg spectral function ($\alpha^2F(\omega)$), and phonon spectra.
important_parameters: Tensile strain (up to $\sim 6\%$ expansion), surface orientation ((001), (110), (111)), and anharmonic renormalization effects.
main_assumptions: The calculated slab systems are idealized, clean, free-standing crystalline reference systems, and the harmonic approximation fails for surfaces, necessitating anharmonic corrections.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate phonon dispersions, $\alpha^2F(\omega)$ for different strain/surface conditions, and comparisons between MLIP-predicted and DFT atomic forces for various Nb structures.
paper_structure: The paper systematically analyzes the effects of strain on bulk Nb, then addresses the necessity of anharmonic corrections for surface slabs, comparing the resulting $T_c$ and EPC strength across different surface terminations.
why_interesting: While focused on BCS-type superconductivity, the rigorous treatment of anharmonic lattice dynamics and the use of MLIPs to accelerate complex many-body calculations are highly relevant to understanding quantum materials far from equilibrium.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Parity plot of MLIP-predicted versus DFT atomic forces for Nb bulk and surface configurations. Each color corresponds to an MLIP trained on a progressively expanded dataset: Bulk+Nb(110) (red), Bulk+Nb(110)+Nb(001) (blue), and Bulk+Nb(110)+Nb(001)+Nb(111) (green). The diagonal line indi- cates perfect agreement.

FIG. 2. (a) Crystal structure of bulk Nb; dashed and solid lines indicate the conventional and primitive unit cells, respectively. (b) Electronic band structure along high-symmetry directions with the total DOS (states/eV) shown on the right; the dashed horizontal line marks the Fermi level (E = 0). (c) Nb 4d orbital-projected Fermi surface colored by the 4d orbital weight (color scale shown below). (d) Harmonic phonon dispersion with the phonon DOS, Eliashberg spectral function α2F(ω), and cumulative EPC constant λ(ω). The line thickness in the dispersion is proportional to the mode-resolved EPC strength. (e) Harmonic phonon dispersions at four lattice constants (a = 3.31 Å being the...

FIG. 3. Eliashberg spectral function α2F(ω) (black) and functional derivative δTc/δα2F(ω) (orange) for bulk Nb at four lattice constants (panels a–d). Vertical lines mark characteristic energy scales: the peak positions of α2F(ω) (αM, black dashed) and δTc/δα2F(ω) (δM, orange dashed), the optimal pairing scale 7kBTc (blue dash-dotted), and the logarithmic average phonon frequency ωlog (green dotted). The lattice constant and calculated Tc for each case are given in the panel insets.

FIG. 4. Side-view atomic structures and corresponding phonon dispersions of Nb slabs: (a) Nb(001), (b) Nb(110), and (c) Nb(111). In each panel, the left subfigure shows the phonon dispersion along high-symmetry directions, while the right subfigure presents the atomic slab structure. Black lines denote harmonic phonon spectra, while red lines represent anharmonic phonon dispersions obtained from SSCHA calculations.

FIG. 5. Temperature dependence of the superconducting gap ∆0 obtained from isotropic Migdal–Eliashberg calculations for the Nb(001), Nb(110), and Nb(111) slabs. Dots are calculated values; solid lines are guides to the eye.
